speaker: interviewer
text: Erna, you were telling us about your day working at the Krupps ammunition factory. Would you just tell us who came to Essen with you-- Essen with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Essen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and two sisters, who-- one of them went to school with me. Their name was Rene and Agnes Konigsberg. They were with us all along. In fact, when-- when my sister and I were picked out to go to work in Essen, they did not pick-- the-- the two of them were-- had typhus in Gelsenkirchen. So they were very sickly. And they were not picked to go to work. And I said to one of the men who picked me out that I am not going unless they can come with us. So he let-- he had them come also. So they came with us. We all stayed together. And we sort of looked out for each other. The four of us stayed together. When, for instance, in sleeping on the floor in the winter was very cold, and since we just had that one dress and one blanket, no coat, but one blanket, so the blanket served as a cover for at night and also as a coat for during the day. And so one blanket was not warm enough. So the four of us would sleep together and put the four blankets together. That way all of us were warm enough, or kept each other warm. And-- we-- one-- one day in the winter, I remember a very snowy, very cold winter, as we-- I mentioned before, I think, that on December 12, there was a bombing. And our camp was bombed. And also in town, the train that we used to take, the streetcar, was bombed. So it was out of commission. So from then on, we had to march to work. We went by foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to get up even earlier, because it was seven kilometers each way. So we marched to work. And one day, my wooden shoes broke as we were marching to work. And there was nothing to do. So we just-- my sister and I stayed behind. We tore off a piece of my blanket and wrapped that around my foot and marched to work, because couldn't escape. There was no place to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the way we looked, everybody would have known right away that we are Jewish prisoners and just couldn't escape. So we just went back to work. And one of the men working in the factory saw that I didn't have shoes. And next day, he brought shoes for me. And they were all-- every once in a while you found some people who were very nice to us. They would bring in a piece of bread or whatever to help us. In fact, one time one of the workers said that he knows of a German family, a German man, who said when time comes, if the girls, if somebody needs help, he's willing to help. But we didn't know how to go about it, until in February, where there was a bombing everywhere again when-- as we were in the camp at night, the SS people who guarded us went into shelters. But we were not allowed to go into shelters. And that's when one of the women-- her name was Rose Katz, who I don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew her before. But she approached my sister and me and said that-- asked rather, if we would be willing to escape. I said, well, where could we go? She says she knows how to get-- where one of the workers from the factory works and he would help us. So my sister and I and the two Konigsberg girls and this Rose Katz and also a sixth girl, I think her name was Gisele Israel, we escaped while the SS guards were in the shelter. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1945, it was February 1945. And we went in to a Jewish cemetery, went into a building there. But then overnight, there was a bombing, and the building was destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went over to another side of the building. And we stayed there. And the next morning, this Rose Katz and my sister went over to the-- where this German lived. Rose Katz apparently knew where this German lived because she had done some work for him and told him that we are hiding, the six of us, and would he help in any way? So he would come over once a day and bring us either a bottle of water or a piece of bread or maybe a potato or whatever he had. But then as we were-- and he said he also would see if he can get in touch with the man who said he knew somebody he would help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then one day as we were hiding out in the cellar of the building in the cemetery, a German came in and asked who we were. We said we were German people. And our house-- we were from Duisburg and our homes were bombed. Our parents were out looking for another place to live. And that's why we are hiding here. And he left. But we were afraid that he might have been from police or even if he wasn't he would probably know that what we told him was not true and he would report to us. So we went over to the German and said, we cannot stay there. He has to-- so he took us to his place. But he was really a very poor man. He didn't have much. And he couldn't keep the six of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Markwad, Mr. Markwad. And he kept us in his place. And then he told us we-- he really cannot feed us. And again, we told him about Mr. Schneider, who would know where this man who wanted to help us lived. So he went to look for Mr. Schneider, but his place was bombed out, his area. But somehow he found him. And he took us to a Mr. Niermann, Fritz Niermann, who took us in, took four of us in. Rose Katz then went to somebody else, to some-- live with somebody else. But the four of us, my sister and two Konigsberg girls and I went to this Mr. Fritz Niermann, who was the most wonderful person I have ever, ever encountered. He took us in. He was a wholesale, food wholesaler. And he had a big home. And separate from his home was an apartment [INAUDIBLE] he used to have rented out. There was no tenant there then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he gave us this apartment or big room. Excuse me. And I remember we took our clothes off and burned it in a little stove that was in the room that he put us in. And he told us-- he opened the closet-- he had his wife and two daughters. And he said, anything you want to take from the closet to wear, whatever you need. And we took out clothes and then burnt our own.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- we took a bath for the first time since we left home. And-- the first time since we left home, we slept in beds on white sheets. And he treated us very, very well. He had a maid who cooked for-- for him. And he would-- she cooked for us also. And every day she would bring in the food for us. And he would keep us informed how the war was going. And he was just wonderful to us, really wonderful. I-- I couldn't imagine that anybody can be this-- because he endanger not only his life but his family's life. Because if he was caught with us hiding in his place, I'm sure that he would have been killed. And not only did he help us, but there were some Russian prisoners in town that he would have his maid cook in barrels and would bring it over to feed these Russian prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't he afraid that the Nazis would caught him collaborating with the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure he was. But it was done very secretly. And nobody knew that we were there. And he [? managed ?] he managed to hide us until liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid with going through so many different people-- you mentioned Mr. Markwad and Mr. Schneider, that somebody in these people's families would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Would report us? Well, we-- we were never-- no, never afraid of-- of being caught and dying. I mean, there was no-- we didn't take many-- there was no risk for us. We knew what awaited us if we didn't escape or what awaited us if we stayed in camp. One of the young SS soldiers always told us, you'll never get out alive. We'll all-- always have the five minutes to kill you all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no risk taking really. I mean, there was no-- the alternative definite death. Mr. Niermann, when he rescued you and put you up in his home, did you have to stay indoors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes we stayed indoors all the time, all the time. Once in awhile he would-- we would go into his apartment like, for instance, on I remember Easter. He would-- we went into his apartment and had lunch at his apartment. And the maid made waffles, which I never in my life had waffles. We had waffles with-- with the strawberries. And-- And he would always take out the map and let us know how the war was-- was going, how things were going. And I guess he was hoping that it would be over soon. And sure enough on-- in April-- I think it was around Easter time that was the very first time that we left the apartment. I guess we looked normal enough by then, because we were wearing his daughter's clothing and some of our hair-- our hair grew back, excuse me, and we went outside. And we saw a Jeep with soldiers in it. We didn't know they were American soldiers. So we ran back in, and we told him that we saw a Jeep, you know, a Jeep with soldiers. And they went out and saw that it was American soldiers, so that's-- that's how they knew. That's where we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for point of reference, where was Mr. Niermann's wife and children at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wife and two daughters were evacuated from Essen because Essen being an industrial town was being bombed a lot. So a lot of the families, whoever could afford it, evacuated their families to other places in-- in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, before liberation, I [INAUDIBLE] just take you back to you worked in that Krupps munition factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On occasion you said you did take things inside. What did you see inside? Who was working there? What can you describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the women from our group were women-- working at ovens. Others were working on cranes. At-- at many different jobs, but inside the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working alongside the Germans at the time? Or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones in the factory were working alongside the Germans. I was working outside. So there were no Germans there, except for the SS people who guarded us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think they used the Jews inside the munitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used them, I suppose, wherever they needed help. Maybe a lot of the young men were in the army or-- during the war. So they were short of young people. And that's why they needed-- that's why they took us to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish men working in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We haven't seen any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any form of-- forms of plans of sabotage by the women knowing that you were working in a Jewish-- in a German munitions plant?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- you know, we actually, I don't know if we knew that it is an ammunition factory, because we didn't actually see any of the guns or ammunition. The finished product we didn't see. So there was no-- no-- no sabotage any kind, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the owner at any point of this munitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean, Krupp? No, no. We didn't. We had-- some of the people in the-- in the ammunition factory, there was one man whose name was, I think, [? Hammerschmidt. ?] And one was Braun. I remember Braun being a very tall man, lame. And he was in a very high position. Now I think that the Braun products that you see now-- you see the Krupp and the Braun product, the coffee makers and whatnot. I think that that Braun was-- apparently has his own Braun factory, or his family does. So that must've been that Braun. But he-- he was about the highest official in the factory who I have seen. But I have not seen the Krupps. Not until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Now I want to take you back to Mr. Niermann's home and liberation. When you told Mr. Niermann about liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did he react?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was very happy. And he said we can stay as long as we want. But, of course, we wanted to report to authorities that we are survivors. And we wanted to somehow get in touch, find out if anybody's alive, who's alive, and-- and possibly get together with somebody from our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think Mr. Niermann took such a chance in feeding--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess, he was just against a war. He was against the Nazis. They-- he realized it was wrong what they were doing. And for humanitarian reasons he just wanted to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think he realized the situation he would be in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure--

speaker: interviewer
text: --had the war not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, I'm sure. Every-- every German had to realize that what-- how dangerous it was to-- to help. That they're risking their own lives and their families' lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay after the actual liberation? You said was in what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in April--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 1945. We didn't stay very long then. We didn't stay long. We stayed long enough until his wife came back and daughters. So we met them. And-- and they were equally nice, wonderful, wonderful people. Wonderful people.

speaker: interviewer
text: No evidence of anti-Semitism, even?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. None at all. They were wonderful people. And-- but we left shortly after the liberation and went to-- my sister and I went to work for the UNRRA. That's the United Nations Relief [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Association, I suppose, or what? I don't what the A stands for. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this? What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Essen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Essen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Essen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do for UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we worked in their house-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- Agnes Konigsberg and I stayed together and work for the UNRRA, kept house for them. And my sister Elizabeth and Rene stayed-- now, I don't remember what they did. I don't remember then what they did. But we-- we separated because the Konigsberg girls didn't want to stay together. They-- they preferred that Agnes stayed with me and-- and Rene stayed with my sister. And we did that for a short time. And then my sister and I got a job working for the North German Coal Control. And we were working as receptionists there. And the two Konigsberg girls went to work at an officer's club in Mulheim, Germany. So we stayed in--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was still in Essen? You were still Essen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still in Essen. And the North German Coal Control was located in [? Kruppvilla. ?] This was a magnificent, magnificent building, gorgeous. And it was mainly a-- an English-- the Essen and-- and the Ruhr area of Germany was occupied by British.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the North German Coal Control was a British organization. And we worked for them and stayed there for quite awhile as receptionists. But then we realized that we wanted to go to-- we found out-- somehow we met-- the-- the Konigsberg girls had a brother who was in Auschwitz with my father. And he found us after the war somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told us that our father, my father, did not survive. He was in Auschwitz, but he did not survive. He died of starvation. And so we decided we had no reason to go back home at all. We knew that our-- the rest of the family died. So we decided to-- we would like to go to America. My brother-- we got in touch with my brother in Israel. And he really wanted us to come to Israel. But at that time, Israel was still under British occupation. It was not-- we could not go there legally. If we went there, it would have been illegally. And there was a lot of fighting going on. And we just did not want to go. Although I was a big Zionist before the war-- and still am-- I did not want to go illegally. And I didn't want to go anywhere where there's still fighting going on. So we decided-- decided we not-- did not want to go to Israel. We'd rather go to United States. Through my brother, we found out our aunt-- we had one aunt in Pittsburgh. We found out her address. And we had written to her. And then she had arranged having sponsored us and sent shipping-- a ticket for the ship so that we could come to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt Helen, Helen [? Schwartz. ?] But since we were in the British occupied area, it was not-- we couldn't go to America from there. So we had to go to a-- to an American occupation area, which was-- so we went Frankfurt then. We went to Frankfurt am Main in Germany because that was under American occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we tried to arrange-- this was in 1946 or '7, I'm not sure. And then we started trying to arrange to go to United States. And I can say now that that wasn't very easy either because there was a quota. And Czechoslovakia did not have a very high quota. So we were told that if we have-- if we say that we are German citizens, German quota's much higher. And would be-- we'd have a much better chance of getting to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to get papers saying that we were German citizens so that we could go to the United States. And we had somehow arranged, got that, arranged that. And that's how we came to the States. Do you know how you-- who you went to? Who put--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to somebody in southern Germany, in Augsburg. And it was-- while we were in Frankfurt, we worked-- I worked at the JCC.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Committee-- Jewish-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forget. Jewish, some kind of committee. Jewish-- I forget what to the second C stands for. Jewish [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the JCC, or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: JCC, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: JCC.

speaker: interviewee
text: JCC. They still have JCCs, what does it stand for? Some Jewish community, but I don't-- Jew-- I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have JCCs, but that stands for Jewish community center here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe that's what it was, Jewish community center. Yeah. And I work for them in Frankfurt. And my sister worked in a-- a Jewish-- like an officer's club, where the American GIs would come to get together with other Jewish GIs. If it was like a-- a club, I suppose. And I guess maybe through that, whether it was through the JCC and being in touch with people, [INAUDIBLE] we found out how to try to get papers that we were German citizens. And I remember going to Augsburg to get the papers. I remember going on a train, standing all the way, because people were traveling with a lot, a lot of packages and whatnot, and trains being very crowded. But we were told how to get in touch with somebody down in Augsburg. And we got the papers. And somehow we managed to get to the United States that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1948. 1948. But before we came to the States, in 19-- it must have been during 19-- end of 1947, just before we came to the States, I was-- we were contacted by some Americans who found out that we were working at the Krupp factories. And they wanted us to testify at the Nuremberg trials. So my sister and I were taken to Nuremberg for the Krupp trials, where we testified for about two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: You became involved with the war crimes trial through the American soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think they found out about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if they were soldiers. They were lawyers. I think it was one of their lawyers from Nuremberg came up and contacted us and asked if we would come and testify.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know this gentleman's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forget. It was a Jewish man. It was a Jewish man, a lawyer. And I forget his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you report to a witness-- that you witnessed an investigative organization before the trial? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, there was no pre-trial investigation of any kind. Nobody asked us any questions. They just wanted us to come and testify at the Nuremberg trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody take your statement prior to your going up to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't to recall anybody doing that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hesitate at all going to testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Not really. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than this Jewish American lawyer, were there any other contacts you had before you actually went to Nuremberg for the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he-- you're finished with the tape, is that it?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can answer that question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Did I have any contact with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other contacts other than this America Jewish lawyer before you went up to Nuremberg for the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
